articles.title: A Map of Levers
articles.summary: This synthesis article maps the actors and interventions that can redirect India's digital infrastructure toward substance—from individual habits and household rules to platform design, investor pressure, regulation, and civic accountability.

articles.title: A Reader's Guide to the Series
articles.summary: Helps readers choose where to start in the series based on their question or role, explains the six arcs, and points only to published articles.

articles.title: Age-Appropriate Design and Protecting Vulnerable Users
articles.summary: Argues for age-appropriate design defaults, friction, and verification to protect children, elderly users, and others who are especially vulnerable to extraction.

articles.title: AI as a Journeyman Assistant, Not a Replacement
articles.summary: Practical guidance on treating AI as a collaborator that drafts, translates, tutors, and debugs, while keeping the human learner in the driver's seat.

articles.title: AI Could Make Extraction Cheaper Too
articles.summary: Surveys how AI can amplify attention extraction through synthetic media, hyper-personalization, and automated influence, and argues for guardrails before scale.

articles.title: Alternative Metrics: Time Well Spent, Sustainability, Learning, Civic Value
articles.summary: Surveys proposed alternatives to engagement-based metrics and their tradeoffs: time well spent, learning outcomes, civic value, and sustainability.

articles.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment: A Series Index
articles.summary: A guide to the Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment series: the diagnosis, the generational stakes, individual practice, systemic design, and curated reading paths.

articles.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment: The TLDR
articles.summary: A concise, shareable summary of the Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment series. It focuses on the productivity opportunity cost of mobile and AI misuse and how individuals can choose substance over scroll.

articles.title: Bhashini and the Indic-Language AI Moment
articles.summary: How Indic-language AI could turn India's language diversity from a barrier into a creative force for substantive content.

articles.title: Business Models That Reward Substance
articles.summary: Compares revenue models and their incentive effects, arguing that business-model diversity is a precondition for design diversity.

articles.title: By the Numbers: What Indians Actually Do Online
articles.summary: Uses government, academic, and industry sources to map India's digital time budget across entertainment, education, work, and communication.

articles.title: Engagement Is a Design Choice
articles.summary: Explores how engagement-based ranking and ad-supported business models are design choices, not natural laws. Cites evidence that alternative metrics can reduce divisive amplification and asks what it would take to make substance the default.

articles.title: Failure, Teaching, and the Skill Stack
articles.summary: Argues that early failure and public teaching are features, not bugs, of building substance over time.

articles.title: Friction, Chronological Feeds, and User-Chosen Algorithms
articles.summary: Surveys evidence on design interventions that reduce extraction: chronological feeds, friction before sharing, and user control over ranking algorithms.

articles.title: Gender and the Attention Economy
articles.summary: Uses government surveys and industry data to examine how women experience India's attention economy differently—through access gaps, unpaid care burdens, online safety risks, and unequal creator-economy opportunities.

articles.title: Historical Analogies of Missed Transitions
articles.summary: Uses cross-country historical comparisons to show that missing a technology transition has persistent economic and geopolitical consequences.

articles.title: Historical Hinges: When Access to a Tool Did Not Guarantee Its Benefits
articles.summary: Uses historical technology transitions to show that access alone does not determine benefit; the distribution of gains depends on institutions, literacy, and norms.

articles.title: India in Global Context: The Attention Economy Is Not Only an Indian Story
articles.summary: Compares India with Brazil, Indonesia, Nigeria, the US, and China on screen time, platform concentration, and regulatory responses.

articles.title: Open Questions the Series Leaves Unresolved
articles.summary: Honest uncertainty about the attention economy—causality, culture, regulation, and the choices that will shape India's next decade.

articles.title: Product Ideas That Could Shift the Incentives
articles.summary: Sketches product and platform alternatives—quality-based incentives, public-interest algorithms, creator reputation economies—that could reward substance over extraction.

articles.title: Public Pressure and Internal Accountability
articles.summary: Maps the ecosystem of non-regulatory accountability: investigative reporting, internal leaks, shareholder pressure, civil-society campaigns, and academic research.

articles.title: Public Space and Private Screens: The Commute Is Not Empty
articles.summary: Uses observational and survey evidence to describe how phones and headphones reshape public space in India, and what incidental conversation, learning, and solidarity are displaced.

articles.title: Regulation as a Floor
articles.summary: Compares India’s IT Rules 2021 and DPDP Act 2023 with the EU DSA, UK OSA, and Australia’s eSafety framework. Regulation can curb harms but cannot alone make substance the easier path; design, business models, and public pressure matter too.

articles.title: Sleep, Anxiety, and the Tele-MANAS Signal
articles.summary: Interprets Tele-MANAS call data and adolescent mental-health surveys as a public-health signal, not moral failure, and connects them to attention-extraction design.

articles.title: The Attention Extraction: How India's Digital Dividend Is Becoming a Cognitive Deficit
articles.summary: A diagnosis of how India's digital-access success has inverted into an attention-extraction economy, using public government, academic, and industry sources to show the costs across attention, productivity, youth mental health, and social trust.

articles.title: Leave Better Than You Arrived: A North Star for Platform Design
articles.summary: Synthesizes the Designing for Substance arc into a practical north star: platform success should be measured by whether users leave with more clarity, skill, or civic capacity than they had when they arrived.

articles.title: The Citizen's Garden: Local Knowledge plus AI
articles.summary: Shows how ordinary citizens can use AI to produce local knowledge and help neighbors, moving from online consumption to offline civic contribution.

articles.title: The Compounding Bet
articles.summary: Reframes India's AI opportunity as a compounding function of attention. Uses Gallup, NASSCOM, and public AI-readiness data to show how small shifts in daily learning, work, and creation habits could aggregate into large differences in national capacity over the next decade.

articles.title: The Creator Economy's Incentive Trap
articles.summary: India's creator economy is reaching massive scale, yet few monetize well and income concentrates at the top. This article examines the gap between aspiration and livelihood, and asks what the AI moment means for young Indians betting on viral attention.

articles.title: The Demographic Dividend Is Not Automatic: Youth, Talent, and Time
articles.summary: Connects India's youth demographics to employment and skill data, arguing that the demographic dividend depends on how young people invest their attention.

articles.title: The Design of Extraction: Variable Rewards, Notifications, and Dark Patterns
articles.summary: Catalogues the interaction-design patterns that keep users in feeds longer than intended, and why each one is a choice, not an inevitable feature of digital life.

articles.title: The Engagement Gap
articles.summary: Examines Gallup engagement data, workplace distraction research, and Indian graduate-employability figures to show how attention extraction affects productivity and the country's demographic dividend.

articles.title: The Family's Garden: Attention Contracts at Home
articles.summary: Offers household-level strategies for managing screens: device-free meals, modeling, shared creative time, and age-appropriate boundaries.

articles.title: The Generational Bet: Will India Build the AI Age or Scroll Through It?
articles.summary: India has the youngest large population on Earth, deep software talent, and cheap AI-assisted tools. The open question is whether the next few years become a compounding bet on building, or a continuation of the attention-economy habit of consuming.

articles.title: The Green Revolution Trade-Off
articles.summary: Uses the Green Revolution as an analogy for solving one urgent problem while creating slower, structural costs that only become visible later.

articles.title: The Indic-Language Internet and the Vernacular Feed
articles.summary: India's internet is now overwhelmingly Indic-language, rural, and mobile-first. This article examines how language expansion multiplied the audience, what vernacular feeds look like at scale, and why engagement-maximizing designs operate across languages with fewer guardrails.

articles.title: The Jio Effect: Cheap Data and the Access-Behavior Paradox
articles.summary: Traces how Jio's 2016 launch collapsed data prices, expanded access, and reshaped Indian internet behavior toward entertainment and short-form video.

articles.title: The Life-Phase Thread
articles.summary: Uses public surveys and academic sources to show that the attention economy imposes different but connected costs at each life stage, from adolescent screen ratios to elderly digital exclusion.

articles.title: The Reel Nation: Short-Form Video and the Economics of a Swipe
articles.summary: Examines the design mechanics and usage scale of short-form video in India, separating documented harms from hype and tracing the opportunity cost.

articles.title: The Small-Rep Theory
articles.summary: Synthesizes research on deliberate practice, deep work, and tiny habits into a practical framework for building substance one small repetition at a time.

articles.title: The Student Screen
articles.summary: A diagnosis of student screen use in India, drawing on ASER 2024 and NCERT 2022. The same device could support learning but is often captured by entertainment and social feeds, with measurable costs to attention, sleep, and mental well-being.

articles.title: The Student's Garden: Learning in Public, Teaching in Private
articles.summary: Offers practical guidance for students using AI: active learning, public note-taking, peer teaching, and avoiding the answer-generation trap.

articles.title: The Worker's Garden: Commutes, Side Projects, and Career Capital
articles.summary: Practical strategies for working adults to reclaim commute and downtime for small acts of learning and creation.

articles.title: Tobacco, Seatbelts, and Food Safety: How Societies Learn to Regulate Design
articles.summary: Surveys public-health precedents to argue that attention-extraction platforms can be treated as design problems, not only user-responsibility problems.

articles.title: Trust and Outrage
articles.summary: Examines the design incentives and evidence behind divisive-content amplification in India, from algorithmic feeds to misinformation and hate-speech trends, and asks whether platforms can be designed to reward cohesion instead of fracture.

articles.title: User Migration and the Exit Problem
articles.summary: Explains why users rarely leave harmful platforms even when alternatives exist, and what interoperability and federation could change.

articles.title: What AI Makes Cheap
articles.summary: Generative AI lowers the cost of writing, coding, translating, designing, tutoring, and many knowledge tasks. This article maps what is becoming cheap, what India is building, and why the payoff depends on whether reclaimed attention flows into learning or back into extraction.

articles.title: What India Is Building vs. What It Could Build
articles.summary: Compares India's current startup and innovation portfolio with the deep-tech and AI infrastructure it could prioritize.

articles.title: Who Profits?
articles.summary: Most of India's digital advertising spending flows to a pair of foreign platforms, while the creator economy is sharply stratified. This article traces the money using regulatory filings and industry research, and asks what a more balanced attention economy would require.
